LinkedIn respects your privacy

LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

  • Top Content

  • Learning
  • Jobs
  • Games
Sign in Join now

Skipping Easy Apply to Boost Job Chances

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer
4mo
  • Report this post

Not gonna lie, I don't use any sort of easy apply features when applying for jobs. Couple of reasons: 1. There can be delays between when a company takes down a posting and when the job comes down from job boards so I may simply be wasting my time. 2. The application on their website may have additional opportunities to answer questions and put a stronger application forward - in a tough job market, I want to make sure every application stands out. 3. Not every  the company has an integration that pushes applications from these websites into their ATS. They may need to manually check these sites to collect the applications, and if they have high volume coming in via their own company job board, they don't have as much of an incentive to do that.  Back when I was doing consulting work, I actually worked with a company that had only nine applications for a position in their ATS, but had over 1,200 applications sitting in Indeed for the exact same position. They had totally forgotten that the job was even pushed to Indeed, meanwhile, nearly every applicant via their own job board got an interview, even ones that were a huge stretch. Or sometimes they have that integration but a glitch prevents apps from being pushed properly. 4. And there's simply a greater risk of falling for a scam. Scammers will post jobs that look real but are actually from a fake company they've set up (with the logo and nearly identical name to a well known company). Does this mean you can't get an interview through these features? Of course now! But there are just a few more things that can go wrong, and to me, it's not worth the risk. And do recruiters hold easy apply apps against candidates? In my experience, no! If they've set it up properly, the apps flow into their ATS automatically and they get reviewed the same as applications from any other source. But if they have to manually log in somewhere else to pull applications, they may simply deprioritize this bucket of applicants. P.S. All credit for the cartoon goes to my buddy Jon Fortenbury - justjoncomic on IG!

65 18 Comments
Like Comment
Elissa Klein 4mo
  • Report this comment

I remember how much Indeed and CareerBuilder pushed using their easy apply. Dude, I typically don’t need MORE applications (except SPED teachers); I need BETTER ones.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Joselyn N. 4mo
  • Report this comment

If I come across a job with the "Easy Apply" option, I always look up the company's career page to find the posting and apply there. However, there have been several times where I haven't been able to find anything and my only option is to apply via LinkedIn. Should I assume that the job posting is a scam? I always click on the company to view their LinkedIn profile and verify they have employees and followers before I apply. I assume if you're able to post a job that links to a particular profile, you must have access to that company's account.

Like
Reply
6 Reactions 7 Reactions
Jonathan Fingas 4mo
  • Report this comment

I’ve also seen a few companies that use Easy Apply to catch lazy applicants — the ones who don't really pay attention. Read the job description and you'll find out that you can only apply through email or a separate web form. It's a bit sneaky, but I also sympathize with recruiters who receive hundreds of applications from people who click without thinking once about their qualifications.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Chi P. 4mo
  • Report this comment

💯 I A/B tested that in the past - when the market was still great. The conversion was 0 for "easy apply" and through "job board". Since then, I go straight to the website of the company to apply.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Jim Coughlin 4mo
  • Report this comment

That's an important distinction that I think gets lost in the conversation between recruiters and jobseekers. The process is so much less automated than people tend to assume, particularly around rejections BUT that does mean that easy apply sources can get overlooked.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction
Joubin Hanaie 4mo
  • Report this comment

That ATS disconnect is real. We see the same thing with background checks. If the integration isn't seamless, stuff just falls through the cracks, and nobody notices until weeks later.

Reply
1 Reaction
Isabel Balogun 4mo
  • Report this comment

I get where you’re coming from and do believe that to be true for most companies but some smaller ones still rely on easy apply. Ironically my last two roles I’ve landed have been through easy apply!

  • Report this comment

The application questions part is always a surprise to job seekers when we discuss this topic!

Kelli Hrivnak 4mo
  • Report this comment

Easy Apply is a poor choice on behalf of employers too.

Christina Swenson, MEd 4mo
  • Report this comment

For some companies I've applied to recently, Easy Apply was the only option. Interestingly enough, the last two hits I have had came through Easy Apply

More Relevant Posts

  • Anmol Chawla
    4mo
    • Report this post

    The biggest trap on LinkedIn is "Easy Apply." In 2022, It was launched as a convenience for the job seeker, but in 2026, companies use it to filter lazy applications. Let's do some basic napkin math here Roles with "Easy Apply" have on average a few 100 applications within a few hours. Let's say 200 for our example in 3 hours and then a day later around 1000 But the hiring manager only looks at the first 20/25 "High Match" profiles after a few hours, that is rougly 2.5% view rate. That means, if you did not apply in the first few hours from the post, you will likely fall with the 97.5% whose application was not even seen. If I had to find another job in this market, I would not bother with the easy application process. I would have two rule of thumbs 1. Velocity > Volume 2. Custom Cold Outreach > Resume customization The "Secret" isn't applying to more jobs. It’s being the first to apply to the right jobs and hyper-focusing on your offering I have compiled an entire repository just focused on getting a call back, because most people don't struggle as much with cracking the interview; they struggle with getting the call back to begin with. It is a top of the funnel problem. Comment "Tools" and I will DM you the resources Note: Ensure we are connected on LinkedIn and send a message; LinkedIn does not allow DM's without connections P.S. repost this to help out fellow job seekers land more interviews :)

    11 17 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Abirami Ravindran
    4mo Edited
    • Report this post

    I Almost Fell for a Job Scam on LinkedIn — And I Work in Recruitment For the past few months, I've been actively interviewing alongside my freelance work. But one experience stood out — not for the right reasons. I received a message from someone claiming to be a recruiter from a well-known MNC with offices in both India, Finland and many other countries. The opportunity looked perfect on paper; the role, location, salary, and JD all matched exactly what I was looking for. She said she found me through my LinkedIn profile. Flattering, right? She invited me for an interview but then dropped this: "Your resume isn't compatible with our ATS tool, but I believe in your skills." Being in recruitment myself, I took that as a challenge. I used LLMs, reworked my CV, and sent it back. Within seconds — an instant reply saying it still wasn't right. She then conveniently offered help and shared a contact for "resume creation services." That's when I started noticing the red flags I had ignored: She was not using the company's official email ID The "resume expert" she referred had no LinkedIn presence The urgency and instant rejection were clearly scripted But here's where it gets personal and complicated. I'm a freelancer. I use my personal email for all professional communication because I simply cannot afford a custom domain. Many genuine independent recruiters, consultants, and freelancers are in the same boat. So when scammers hide behind personal emails, they don't just steal money or data — they steal trust. And that stolen trust falls hardest on people like us, who were never doing anything wrong in the first place. If this almost fooled me — someone who works in recruitment — what chance does a first-time job seeker have? And what chance do genuine freelancers have when every personal email is now a reason for suspicion? I'm not sharing this out of embarrassment. I'm sharing this because we look out for each other. The right door will open and you won't have to force it.

    22 5 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Shyna Menon©
    4mo
    • Report this post

    LinkedIn Model Acting as Abuser I want to highlight several serious issues happening across professional recruitment funnels and platforms: 1️⃣ Exploitation of Work & Identity • Unauthorized use of my ideas, designs, or professional identity for personal or commercial gain is illegal and punishable. • Claiming I manage teams I don’t know or represent, for someone else’s benefit, constitutes misrepresentation and passing off, actionable under Trademark, Business, and Anti-Fraud laws. • Mocking, insulting, or calling me names under the guise of a “joke” is defamation and harassment, also legally actionable. 2️⃣ Pay-for-Action vs. Pay-for-Visibility • Paying for CV visibility or “being in the funnel” without real recruitment is exploitative and non-standard. • The credible model, even for unregistered consultants, is: Payment only once a candidate is successfully recruited and salary is credited. • Any advance fees for stagnant promises or funnel presence are abusive and unprofessional. 3️⃣ Funnel & Notification Accountability • Recruitment and candidate funnels must include real-time tracking, stage notifications, and pay-for-result alignment. • Stagnant applications or manipulative processes without notifications are unacceptable. 4️⃣ Manipulation via Irrelevant Jobs & Paywalls • Platforms are using irrelevant job postings, long forms, and engagement gimmicks to force users toward paid services. • This is manipulation, not professional service. Candidates and organizations deserve systems that work efficiently and transparently. 5️⃣ Legal Framework (for reference) • Copyright Act, 1957: Sections 14, 51, 63, 63A, 64 • Trade Marks Act, 1999: Sections 27, 29(6–8), 102, 103, 104, 105 • Indian Penal Code (IPC): Sections 420, 464, 499, 500 • Indian Contract Act: Sections 18, 19 • Civil remedies: Passing off, injunctions, damages Takeaway: Professional platforms, consultants, and organizations should respect IP, professional identity, and fair funnel practices, provide transparent tracking, and only monetize real recruitment results. Anything else is exploitation. Now let’s imitate LinkedIn I have already started installing paywall to access my time!

    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Maride Dean
    4mo
    • Report this post

    Scam reminder for job seekers. If someone messages you saying your resume “failed the ATS,” or that you “only scored 52%,” and then offers to “fix it for a small fee,” pause. That is a very common scam pattern. Here is what is true: Most ATS platforms are filing and search systems. They store applications, parse your resume into fields, and help recruiters search and filter candidates. There is no universal ATS score that every company uses, and legitimate recruiters do not require you to pay money to be considered. Here are the red flags I see most often: A random message right after you apply Urgency like “submit today or lose the role” A payment link or referral to a paid “ATS rewrite” service A generic job description with no verifiable company trail A recruiter profile with thin history or mismatched details What to do instead: Verify the role on the company’s official careers page Check that the recruiter has a real company email domain Ask for the job ID and hiring team name Do not share sensitive personal data until the employer is confirmed One more nuance: “ATS-friendly” formatting is real. Clean structure helps systems parse your resume and helps humans scan it. But “pay us because your resume is not ATS compliant” is usually a pressure tactic. Protect your time. Protect your money. Protect your momentum. If you want, comment “CHECK” and I will share a quick checklist of safe resume formatting and recruiter verification steps.

    15 14 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Vrijen Attawar
    3mo Edited
    • Report this post

    More on this in a few weeks, but Bonnie is absolutely correct. Fundamentally, ATS systems look at keywords not qualities. Even if we're being charitable, this means that they can catch some wavelengths of candidate signal, but not all, and certainly not ones that carry soft skills, values, or mindset info. If there's one upside to all this, it's that the fact that the emperor has no clothes cannot be ignored. The future is being ably demonstrated companies like Zapier that are setting up their own pipeline and flows (albeit with the advantage of using their own Tech to power it). Companies and VCs that own their own talent pipeline will come to see it as a major competitive advantage when they aren't paying 15% or higher per linchpin hire. This is why I firmly believe that it will take major litigation or federal legislation. God help us all until then.

    Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer

    Recruiting Leader @ Zapier | Former Educator | I’m a fan of transparency in recruiting, leveraging AI to make work more efficient and human, and workplaces that work for everyone.

    3mo

    Your goal as an applicant is to "beat the ATS". At least that's what I hear across social media. But this idea is largely a myth perpetuated by two groups: 1. ATS companies who for the last 2 decades have been making claims about the sophistication of their tech to justify enterprise pricing. And you can see this is true when you actually start asking questions about the AI they're using and it quickly becomes clear that it's akin to CTRL+F. There ARE some innovative GAI solutions/features being built now, but it's early, very few companies are using them, and reputable companies offer disclosure and the ability to opt in/out. 2. Resume writers, job search "experts", and influencers who profit off of selling services to "beat the ATS" and the virality that comes with "us against the system" content. And again, it becomes really clear that this is more of a narrative for virality when you start looking at the backgrounds of the people giving this advice and in many cases, they've never worked in spaces where they've actually been involved in hiring. Most of the people with actual recruiting expertise are not pushing these narratives because they understand the tools. Let's logic check this: Do people actually believe that companies are paying hundreds of thousands of dollars a year for tools that make it harder for them to hire qualified people? And right now, companies aren't struggling to find great talent. They are hiring people they are really excited about, which means the systems are meeting their needs. So then why are so many great people having a hard time in this market? Well, that would go back to our good friend, supply and demand. Analysis of application data on LinkedIn showed that over half of applications are submitted for remote jobs which made up just 13% of the jobs listed. Things are getting more expensive, and people feel stagnant in their roles so lots of people are applying for the ever-shrinking number of really appealing, high-paying jobs. Companies have their pick of talent. And with that growing applicant volume AND increasing candidate fraud, many companies are leaning more heavily on referrals, networks, or people who go above and beyond to stand out. They are leaning on more ways to filter people OUT of their process with requirements like videos, agentic interviews, and more complex application questions. They are relying more on sourcing perfect-fit candidates vs relying solely on inbound apps. A slightly more "optimized" (whatever that means) resume isn't gonna cut it. Mass applying with low quality applications for stretch jobs isn't gonna cut it. Refusing to provide work samples or answer application questions or engage with an agentic screener isn't gonna cut it. What is going to cut it is: Applying for jobs you are objectively qualified for with really strong applications that stand out, leaning on your existing networks, and targeting fields where job creation is happening.

    8 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Bonnie Dilber Bonnie Dilber is an Influencer

    Your goal as an applicant is to "beat the ATS". At least that's what I hear across social media. But this idea is largely a myth perpetuated by two groups: 1. ATS companies who for the last 2 decades have been making claims about the sophistication of their tech to justify enterprise pricing. And you can see this is true when you actually start asking questions about the AI they're using and it quickly becomes clear that it's akin to CTRL+F. There ARE some innovative GAI solutions/features being built now, but it's early, very few companies are using them, and reputable companies offer disclosure and the ability to opt in/out. 2. Resume writers, job search "experts", and influencers who profit off of selling services to "beat the ATS" and the virality that comes with "us against the system" content. And again, it becomes really clear that this is more of a narrative for virality when you start looking at the backgrounds of the people giving this advice and in many cases, they've never worked in spaces where they've actually been involved in hiring. Most of the people with actual recruiting expertise are not pushing these narratives because they understand the tools. Let's logic check this: Do people actually believe that companies are paying hundreds of thousands of dollars a year for tools that make it harder for them to hire qualified people? And right now, companies aren't struggling to find great talent. They are hiring people they are really excited about, which means the systems are meeting their needs. So then why are so many great people having a hard time in this market? Well, that would go back to our good friend, supply and demand. Analysis of application data on LinkedIn showed that over half of applications are submitted for remote jobs which made up just 13% of the jobs listed. Things are getting more expensive, and people feel stagnant in their roles so lots of people are applying for the ever-shrinking number of really appealing, high-paying jobs. Companies have their pick of talent. And with that growing applicant volume AND increasing candidate fraud, many companies are leaning more heavily on referrals, networks, or people who go above and beyond to stand out. They are leaning on more ways to filter people OUT of their process with requirements like videos, agentic interviews, and more complex application questions. They are relying more on sourcing perfect-fit candidates vs relying solely on inbound apps. A slightly more "optimized" (whatever that means) resume isn't gonna cut it. Mass applying with low quality applications for stretch jobs isn't gonna cut it. Refusing to provide work samples or answer application questions or engage with an agentic screener isn't gonna cut it. What is going to cut it is: Applying for jobs you are objectively qualified for with really strong applications that stand out, leaning on your existing networks, and targeting fields where job creation is happening.

    153 63 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • VINAY CHAUBEY
    4mo
    • Report this post

    How to Spot Fake Job Posts on LinkedIn: A Guide for Job Seekers LinkedIn has become one of the most powerful platforms for professional networking and job search. But with the rise of remote work and digital hiring comes an increase in fake or fraudulent job postings — advertisements designed to collect personal data, lure applicants into scams, or waste time with illegitimate opportunities. Understanding how to identify these fake job posts can save you time, protect your personal information, and help you focus on real opportunities that match your skills and goals. 🔎 1. Check the Company Page Carefully Legitimate job posts are almost always linked to an official company page. Inspect the company’s LinkedIn profile: Is it complete with logo, website link, and company description? Do they have recent posts and employee activity? If the company profile looks new, empty, or has minimal content, be cautious. Tip: Search for the company name online and confirm details like website, reviews, and location. 🧑💼 2. Look at Who Posted It Genuine job posts are usually published by HR professionals or recruiters — often with a complete LinkedIn profile. Check the poster’s experience, connection count, profile consistency, and endorsements. Fake posters often use generic names, lack professional history, or have very few connections. 💼 3. Analyze the Job Description Real job descriptions tend to have: Clear role responsibilities and required qualifications Details about company values and the team Precise location or remote details Well-written language Red flags to spot: ❌ Vague descriptions like “work from anywhere” with no clear job duties ❌ Overly high salary promises with minimal requirements ❌ Generic titles such as “Customer Support Specialist — Immediate hiring!” 📩 4. Beware of Suspicious Contact Methods Legitimate employers rarely ask candidates to: Contact them through personal email addresses (e.g., Gmail or Yahoo) Message them on WhatsApp, Telegram, or other social platforms to apply Share sensitive personal information (like bank details or Aadhaar) during initial stages Safe practice: Genuine recruiters will usually communicate through LinkedIn messages or professional emails tied to the company domain. 💰 5. Avoid “Too Good to Be True” Offers If a job sounds unbelievably easy with a very high salary, no experience needed, and guaranteed acceptance, it might be a scam. Scammers attract applicants with unrealistic promises to gather data or extract upfront fees (e.g., for training or software). 📊 6. Look for Multiple Job Listings & Reviews Check if the same job is listed on other platforms like company career pages, Indeed, or Naukri. Genuine jobs are likely to appear across reputable job boards. Search for company reviews on Glassdoor or similar sites to see what current and former employees say. 🤝 7. Recognize Connection Requests with Hidden Motives

    5
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • John Menear
    4mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Part 1 of 3: Resume ATS: When is a Job Referral, not a Job Referral? How much this time? - Using someone else's ID I have an 'Open to Work' on my LinkedIn profile.  It's to let those who are looking for someone to assist in a project know that I am available, even if I am on a contract, using flexible hours / remote work. Based on an agreed schedule, I have had up to 3 contracts running at the same time. Since placing the 'Open to Work', I have received numerous contacts with job postings, or those advising me that positions are available at company 'X', and here is the point of contact.  I received a referral around 1 week ago, and sent an appropriate email to the person concerned. I did not pay attention to the email address, as it was a referral from a person who worked at the company.  Looking back, this is off course; this is where I made the fatal mistake, maybe because I was too trusting, or maybe because I was optimistic. I should have looked at the 'verification' on the person's profile. I emailed the party concerned, advising that I had been referred to them for a potential Database Administration role. The person replied with a job description and their contact information, which appeared to be correct in every way.  Company name, Name, title, etc., and a link to the LinkedIn profile. Again, this is where I made a fatal mistake. I did not open and verify the person's 'verification' details. The resume My resume was requested, and I sent across the latest version 169.1 I received an email with a list of issues regarding the resume and how it was not ATS-compliant. I then sent across a government-formatted resume, which had been created for a specific role for a government agency in the format the government requested. This was only 3 pages.  Very brief high-level overview. Basically, can you do a given job, YES/NO, and provide details of the project and outcome. Again, I was advised it was ATS-compliant; however failed to meet the requirements, as there was insufficient information included. Odd, since this was the information that had been requested for a government agency and successfully submitted by the agency.  Government Resumes / Grids For those who have filled out resumes/grids for the government, they can be extremely detailed, and in certain cases, extremely long. For one government role, I had applied for the resume/grid, which, when filled out, was 58 pages long and took me 8 days to fill out  (average of 10+ hours a day), and another was a spreadsheet with between 15-20 tabs, with x hundred lines, which took 3 days to fill out, as a lot of the details were the same as the 58-page one.

  • Eric Bruder
    4mo
    • Report this post

    r.i.p. the "easy apply" button. poozle just made it obsolete i've tested every job board for the past year. linkedin, indeed, glassdoor, ziprecruiter, etc... they all have the same problem: by the time you see a job, 800+ people already applied. your resume is dead on arrival. then i built poozle and the math changed completely. poozle doesn't show you "more jobs." it pre-qualifies you for roles where you're actually 1 of 30, not 1 of 800. here's what makes it different: • pre-qualification matching: we analyze your background and only show you jobs where you're statistically likely to land an interview • 50-applicant cap: we stop showing roles once they hit 50 applicants so you're never 1 of 800 • swipe to apply: find a match - swipe right. not interested - swipe left. applying takes 2 seconds, not 15 minutes • fresh jobs only: we index directly from company career pages so you see roles in the first 0-72 hours, not week-old postings this is a battle-tested system that: • gets you in front of hiring managers before the flood hits • keeps you in manageable applicant pools where you can actually be seen • eliminates the spreadsheet nightmare (we track everything for you) job seekers are landing interviews 4x faster because they stopped applying to black holes. here's what the difference looks like: applying on linkedin: • find a job with 847 applicants • spend 30 minutes tailoring your resume • submit into the void • get auto-rejected or ghosted • repeat the cycle applying on poozle: • open the app • see each pre-qualified match • swipe right on the ones that fit • you're applicant number 12, 8, and 23 • get interview requests within a week same resume. same skills. 30x better odds. the problem isn't you. it's the pool you're swimming in. i'm giving away free poozle pro access to the next 100 people. if you're tired of the linkedin black hole, this is your exit. comment "SWIPE" below (make sure we're connected so i can DM you) and i'll send you the download link + a code for free pro access. available on ios and android. p.s. if you've applied to 100+ jobs with zero interviews, it's not a resume problem. it's a targeting problem. poozle fixes that.

    142 702 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • George Watson
    4mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Sign of the crooked times... (NB 'Someone' has acquired hacking software that allows the con artist to highjack the profile message address of LinkedIn members and send bogus messages at will.) Just received a legitimate looking LinkedIn contact message from a senior recruiting executive recommending a job opportunity with another recruiter in the same F500 company. While numerous bogus invitations arrive on a regular basis, it looked legitimate enough - both profiles on LI were 'real', so I responded to the questionable email address. I was quickly advised my resume was inadequate for processing and if I just had a professional they were recommending fix it, I would then be considered for the position. Same scam, more advanced approach. Usually the con is just to recommend a person who will improve my chances at getting hired with a resume consultation and a name to contact. Needless to say, I demurred and wished the scam artist well and moved on. But the ploy included what I assume was an actual Ai critique of my resume - a quick list of some of the improvements I needed to make, and it reminded me that the danger in Ai vetted resumes is that it's just an even cheaper way to not properly evaluate candidate resumes. So, just in case you think your resume needs work: 1. Bullet Point Length – Some of the bullet points on your resume were longer than the ideal two-line length. ATS systems are designed to favor concise, scannable content, and longer sections can be misread or overlooked, impacting how your experience is ranked. For example, your descriptions under Creative Career Management and ECA Corporation contain dense paragraphs that could be broken into cleaner, results-focused statements. 2. Experience Format – Your consulting and entrepreneurial experience is highly valuable, but the way it’s currently presented may have been misinterpreted by the system as a gap in employment. ATS platforms often lack the nuance to understand freelance, contract, or founder roles properly, which can inadvertently lower your score. Clearly labeling roles with "Founder," "Consultant," or "Contract" and including company names and date formats consistently will help. 3. Missing Strategic Keywords – Certain key terms, such as specific role-related skills (e.g., "Employer Branding," "Talent Acquisition Strategy," "HR Business Partner") or mentions of company alignment (e.g., “passion for brand storytelling,” “experience scaling organizations”), were missing. While it may feel robotic to include them, these systems are keyword-driven and use such cues to determine cultural fit and relevance. Your background includes impressive brand work with Fortune 500 companies framing that with modern terminology will make a significant difference. So there you have it. My lack of modern terminology or 'jargon' is holding me back in a 20 year career in Executive Recruiting. (Not age, like everyone else. 🤔) Carry On! Smoke, Mirrors and those who prey on the victims

    2
    Like Comment
